entries.summary: Druckenmiller argues 'contrarianism is overrated' — staying long US equities while hedging the dollar via commodities and international exposure. He remains AI-centric ('all that matters is AI and Nvidia'), pairing that equity conviction with a structural dollar-bearish stance expressed through copper and a notable South American macro trade (Brazil's R$33B institutional inflow trade). Duquesne is up 9.8% YTD and 28.4% over the past year, with portfolio shifts tilting away from broad domestic growth toward AI-infrastructure and international opportunities.
key_views: 'Contrarianism is overrated' — stay long US equities, don't fight the AI rally
All that matters is AI and Nvidia — tech dominance driving compounding returns
Structurally bearish on the US dollar; long assets that benefit from USD weakness
South American macro trade: Brazil institutional inflow play (R$33B) in natural resources
Copper long as explicit USD hedge alongside commodities
trade_ideas: Long US equities (AI/tech focused)
Long copper as USD hedge
Long Brazil assets (R$33B institutional inflow theme)

entries.summary: Dalio (now managing his family office after fully exiting Bridgewater) continues to warn investors are entering the most dangerous phase of the 'Big Cycle,' citing 500 years of history showing converging debt crises, political disorder, and geopolitical conflict. He frames the 2026 environment as one of 'capital wars' and 'modern mercantilism,' with tariffs potentially creating 1-2% GDP drag alongside 4-5% sticky inflation — a stagflation trap he sees as highly dangerous for the Fed. Dalio advocates diversifying away from US dollar assets toward gold, real assets, and non-US equities.
key_views: 'Most dangerous phase of the Big Cycle' — debt, disorder, and geopolitical conflict converging for first time in 500 years
'Capital wars' replacing globalization — national self-interest and modern mercantilism dominant
Tariff stagflation: 1-2% GDP drag + 4-5% sticky inflation could force Fed into policy trap
AI arms race adds systemic risk on top of already-stressed fiscal/monetary architecture
No longer at Bridgewater; managing permanent family office capital
trade_ideas: Diversify away from US dollar assets — non-US equities and bonds
Long gold and real assets as Big Cycle hedge

entries.summary: Jones said on CNBC (May 7, 2026) that the AI-fueled bull market has 'another year or two to run,' drawing explicit parallels to 1999 — roughly a year before the dot-com peak. He recently bought more AI-related stocks, viewing the AI era similarly to Microsoft's early software dominance and the 1990s internet build-out. However, he warned that when the cycle ends, corrections will be 'breathtaking' if markets go up another 40% first, and he raised concerns about AI's long-term systemic risks requiring government regulation.
key_views: AI bull market has 'another year or two to run' — 1999 parallel, one year before dot-com peak
Bought more AI-related stocks recently — conviction in near-term upside
Warns of 'breathtaking corrections' when AI bubble ends if markets extend another 40%
AI regulation inevitable — long-term systemic/existential risk concerns
AI parallels Microsoft 1980s and internet 1990s — years of productivity gains ahead
trade_ideas: Long AI-related equities with active monitoring for bubble signals
Maintain hedges given 'breathtaking' correction risk at extended levels

entries.summary: Marks's Oaktree 2026 conference highlighted that direct lending has become commoditized, with alpha migrating to more complex strategies: asset-backed finance and distressed credit. His recent AI memo argues AI is unambiguously transformative — 'far from a fad' — but urges investors to weigh that transformational potential against current AI asset valuations, which he views as a critical and underappreciated variable. Marks continues to emphasize exploiting mispricings created by complexity, forced selling, and market misunderstandings rather than relying on efficient market pricing.
key_views: Direct lending alpha has disappeared as capital flooded post-2011 — pivot to asset-backed finance and distressed credit
AI is transformative and 'far from a fad' — immense power, speed, and autonomy vs prior tech waves
AI asset prices must be weighed against transformational potential — valuation discipline essential
Mispricings arise from complexity, forced selling, supply-demand imbalances, and misunderstandings
Superior thinking, not efficient market reliance, is the edge in private credit
trade_ideas: Long asset-backed finance and distressed credit over commoditized direct lending
Selective AI-adjacent equities where valuation supports transformational thesis

entries.summary: Appaloosa Management surged 32% in H1 2026 on memory chip bets, with all gains generated in Q2 (Bloomberg, July 6). Tepper's Q1 2026 13F portfolio shrank to ~$5.93B across 31 highly concentrated positions — top holdings AMZN, MU, GOOGL, UBER, and TSM — reflecting exit of nearly all bank stocks and a 200% increase in Micron as a high-bandwidth memory AI pick-and-shovel play. Despite the strong performance, Tepper maintains an average 40% cash position, signaling conservative risk management amid AI-driven returns.
key_views: Appaloosa up 32% H1 2026 — all gains in Q2, driven by memory chip sector
High-conviction AI memory play: Micron (HBM) as primary AI pick-and-shovel position, increased 200%
Exited nearly all bank stocks in Q4 2025 — structural rotation away from financials
High concentration: AMZN, MU, GOOGL, UBER, TSM as top 5 holdings in $5.93B portfolio
40% average cash position — conservative risk management despite strong performance
trade_ideas: Long Micron (MU) as AI high-bandwidth memory infrastructure play
Long AMZN, GOOGL, TSM, UBER (top concentration)

entries.summary: Gundlach stated in May 2026 that it is 'just not possible' for the Fed to cut rates — and would bet on hikes instead — citing the 2-year Treasury trading ~50bps above the Fed funds rate and persistently sticky inflation. He proposed a specific portfolio architecture: 30% non-US equities, 20% real assets (10% gold, 5% commodities, 5% other), 30% high-quality bonds, and 20% cash. His core macro thesis is 'dollar negative' — non-US equities are the most important structural overweight — paired with gold as the primary real asset anchor.
key_views: 'Just not possible' for Fed to cut rates in 2026 — would bet on rate hikes instead
2Y Treasury ~50bps above Fed funds rate makes cuts unjustifiable; inflation remains sticky
Dollar structurally bearish — non-US equities (30%) as primary overweight
Gold 10% and commodities 5% as real asset anchors against dollar weakness and inflation
30% high-quality bonds + 20% cash as defensive foundation
trade_ideas: Long non-US equities (30% allocation)
Long gold (10%) and commodities (5%) as real asset anchors
Underweight US dollar assets; avoid duration in inflation environment

entries.summary: Burry's Scion Asset Management fully exited China (Alibaba, JD.com, Baidu liquidated in one move — a macro statement) while initiating a new long in MercadoLibre (MELI), marking a return to Latin American e-commerce. He maintains large put options on Nvidia and Palantir, expressing deep AI-valuation skepticism even as the rally continues. The SaaS rotation is his constructive bet: Scion is accumulating beaten-down SaaS names (Adobe, Autodesk, Veeva) targeting low stock-based compensation, strong 'owner earnings,' and AI productivity beneficiaries.
key_views: Full China exit: BABA, JD.com, Baidu all liquidated simultaneously — decisive macro move
AI skeptic: large put options on Nvidia and Palantir against the AI rally
New long: MercadoLibre (MELI) — return to Latin American e-commerce after prior exit
SaaS rotation: Adobe, Autodesk, Veeva as beaten-down AI productivity beneficiaries
Selective bullishness within cautious macro stance — portfolio ~$68M (personal vehicle post-deregistration)
trade_ideas: Long MercadoLibre (MELI)
Long Adobe, Autodesk, Veeva (beaten-down SaaS)
Short Nvidia and Palantir via puts

entries.summary: Brevan Howard's $11.2B BH Master Fund is generating equity-driven gains that are offsetting losses in rates and FX — a structural shift in where macro alpha is found. The firm wound down the $1.4B FG Fund by end-June 2026 and saw Tekmerion Capital spin out with $1B+. Howard is simultaneously expanding the macro trading team and positioning Brevan as a broader alternative manager, with its Brevan Howard Digital unit managing billions in digital assets. The pivot reflects a view that traditional rates/FX macro is less productive while equities and digital assets offer richer opportunities.
key_views: Equity trading gains now offsetting rates/FX losses — macro alpha has migrated from traditional strategies
$1.4B FG Fund closed June 2026 — eliminating less-differentiated strategies
Tekmerion Capital spinout: $1B+ commitment signals talent migration to independent platforms
Expanding macro team despite mixed H1 — conviction in macro opportunity set intact
Digital assets expansion: Brevan Howard Digital managing billions — structural crypto bet
trade_ideas: Equities as primary macro return driver (replacing rates)
Digital assets via dedicated Brevan Howard Digital unit

entries.summary: Treasury Secretary Bessent reiterated his '3-3-3' framework — 3% GDP growth, 3% deficit-to-GDP, and 3M bpd domestic oil production — and predicted GDP with 'a three in front of it' before year-end (CNBC, June 24). He testified to the Senate that the economy remains strong and on track, explicitly rejecting recession fears and calling 2026 a 'blockbuster year.' His broader thesis frames Treasury's role as economic statecraft — directing American capital toward sectors deemed critical to national security and long-term competitiveness.
key_views: 3-3-3 framework: 3% GDP growth, 3% deficit-to-GDP, 3M bpd domestic oil production
GDP growth 'with a three in front of it' achievable before year-end (CNBC, June 24)
No US recession in 2026 — 'blockbuster year' prediction maintained
Economic statecraft: Treasury directing capital toward national-security-critical manufacturing and tech
Iran energy shock will prove transitory; Fed's 2% inflation target achievable
trade_ideas: Long US equities on growth and deregulation narrative
Long domestic energy production (3M bpd increase policy)

entries.summary: Ackman called current conditions 'one of the best times in a long time to buy quality stocks,' arguing that some of the highest-quality businesses in the world are trading at 'stupidly cheap' prices with potential for 10x returns. His macro framework sees inflation as bullish for quality compounders, GDP slowing but not contracting, and rates staying higher for longer — a backdrop he views as creating mispriced opportunities. He characterizes Pershing Square as 'constructive on the current economic and market backdrop,' launched Pershing Square USA in April 2026, and continues to accumulate long-duration quality compounders.
key_views: 'Best time in a long time to buy quality' — some high-quality businesses 'stupidly cheap'
Macro framework: inflation bullish for quality compounders, GDP slowing but not contracting
Higher-for-longer rates creating mispricings in long-duration quality businesses
Pershing Square 'constructive on the current economic and market backdrop'
Pershing Square USA IPO launched April 2026 ($5B) — expanding to retail investors
trade_ideas: Long high-quality compounders trading at discounts (Alphabet, Amazon, Meta cited)
Pershing Square USA provides retail access to Ackman's concentrated quality positions

entries.summary: Einhorn's Greenlight Capital closed to new investors as of July 1, 2026, signaling the firm sees elevated downside risk in US equities — 'the most expensive we've seen since we began managing money and arguably in the history of the United States.' Capital preservation is now Einhorn's top priority, willing to miss upside to be positioned offensively 'should one of the downside scenarios materialize.' All of Greenlight's 2025 alpha came from the macro portfolio (14.4% gross contribution), with notable wins including Lebanese debt at 66% IRR; for 2026 Einhorn prefers direct exposures to gold, copper, and rates over equities.
key_views: US equities 'most expensive we've seen since we began managing money' — historically extreme valuations
Closed to new investors July 1, 2026 — limiting inflows as downside risk elevated
Capital preservation is top priority — willing to miss upside to play more offense on downturn
All 2025 alpha from macro portfolio (14.4% gross): gold, copper, rates, Lebanese debt (66% IRR)
Prefers direct exposure to gold, copper, rates over equity proxies
trade_ideas: Long gold directly
Long copper directly
Long rates (bonds)
Event-driven credit (Lebanese debt restructuring)

entries.summary: Reid published a major note on July 7-8, 2026 warning that AI productivity gains are 'years away' from materializing in economic data, creating a critical debt risk: if AI doesn't deliver on its productivity promise, already-unsustainable global debt levels worsen significantly. He frames this as the central bull/bear divide of 2026: bulls hope AI becomes 'a productivity miracle you can grow into debt'; bears warn that if rates lift much above current levels, debt sustainability becomes problematic. Deutsche Bank's 2026 theme is 'never a dull moment' — volatile boom-bust narrative swings, with Germany's fiscal rebound and China's moderation as key regional divergences.
key_views: AI productivity gains are 'years away' — economic embedding of AI takes time despite immense potential (July 7-8 note)
Debt sustainability risk: if AI underdelivers, unsustainable global debt levels worsen materially
Bull/bear 2026 divide: AI as productivity miracle to grow into debt (bulls) vs rates rise + debt crisis (bears)
2026 theme: 'never a dull moment' — markets will swing sharply between boom-bust narratives
Germany fiscal rebound major; China moderating on anti-involution reforms; Fed 2 more cuts then pause

entries.summary: Slok's mid-year 2026 outlook identifies three forces driving the economy: the AI data center buildout, an industrial renaissance, and the One Big Beautiful Bill fiscal stimulus — together adding ~2.2 percentage points to GDP per CBO estimates. He notes the economy is 'resilient and doing quite well,' but is increasingly worried about overheating rather than stagflation: inflation could start moving up in H2 2026 as capacity tightens. Consumer and business spending both remain strong, and data center financing is already committed, providing multi-quarter AI-capex momentum.
key_views: Three growth forces: AI data center buildout (+1% GDP), industrial renaissance (+0.3%), fiscal stimulus/OBBB (+0.9%)
Risk has shifted from stagflation to OVERHEATING — inflation could move up in H2 2026
Economy 'resilient and doing quite well' — consumer and business spending both strong
Data center financing already committed — AI capex has multi-quarter momentum regardless of sentiment
K-shaped divergence: headline strength masking significant sector and income disparities

entries.summary: Hartnett's Bull & Bear Indicator hit 9.5 for multiple consecutive weeks as of early July 2026 — a contrarian sell signal — while global equity funds attracted $56.6B in the week ending July 8 (4th-largest weekly inflow of 2026). He calls the current AI market the 'largest bubble since the 1880s railroad boom,' with Mag 7 concentration at 48% of S&P 500 exceeding all historical bubble peaks. Japan bank stocks are flagged as the 'canary' for a global sell-off. His post-bubble playbook recommends 25/25/25/25 stocks/bonds/cash/commodities — the best-performing allocation since 1933.
key_views: Bull & Bear Indicator at 9.5 — contrarian sell signal for multiple consecutive weeks
AI market = largest bubble since 1880s railroad boom; Mag 7 at 48% of S&P concentration
$56.6B weekly equity inflows (week ending July 8) — 4th-largest of 2026 despite sell signals
Japan bank stocks = 'canary' — rising JGB yields could trigger global risk sell-off
Post-bubble playbook: 25/25/25/25 stocks/bonds/cash/commodities best since 1933
trade_ideas: Reduce equity concentration; shift toward 25/25/25/25 stocks/bonds/cash/commodities
Monitor Japan bank stocks as early warning for global correction

entries.summary: Edwards has pivoted his long-running 'Ice Age' thesis (secular stagnation, falling bond yields) toward fiscal dominance and the risk of double-digit inflation returning (Bloomberg, May 15, 2026). He argues governments, constrained by electoral pressures, are unable to unwind fiscal expansion, creating unsustainable debt-to-GDP trajectories in the US and UK. The underlying consumer is 'tapped out' — low savings, rising costs — threatening corporate margins even as AI-driven equity gains persist. This is a major evolution: Edwards now warns of an inflationary fiscal-dominance regime, not the deflationary Ice Age he long predicted.
key_views: Ice Age thesis has evolved: fiscal dominance + double-digit inflation risk now the dominant concern (May 2026)
Governments unable to unwind fiscal expansion due to electoral constraints — unsustainable US and UK debt trajectories
Underlying consumer 'tapped out' — low savings and rising costs threatening corporate margins
AI equity gains unsustainable if consumer spending collapses under cost pressures
Regime shift: from deflationary Ice Age to inflationary fiscal dominance — major thesis evolution
trade_ideas: Avoid long-duration bonds — fiscal dominance creates persistent inflation risk premium
Long inflation protection: TIPS, commodities, real assets

entries.summary: Rosenberg frames 2026 as a 'Global Restructuring' — not a mere recession or correction but a fundamental reorder of the kind that looks transformative in 15 years' hindsight. He characterizes the economy as 'Bipolar' — extreme data swings driven by tariff front-running, government shutdowns, and geopolitical shocks — with GDP growth averaging 2.7% YoY through March 2026 but Q4 2025 GDP at just 0.5%. His Rosie Macro Fund is up 28% YTD vs S&P 500 at 14%, validating his contrarian strategy; he expects pronounced disinflation to force the Fed to cut more aggressively than markets currently price.
key_views: 'Global Restructuring' — fundamental 15-year reorder, not a recession; geopolitics redefining economics
'Bipolar Economy' — extreme data swings from tariff front-running, shutdowns, and geopolitical shocks
GDP averaging 2.7% YoY through March 2026, but Q4 2025 GDP only 0.5% (government shutdown impact)
Disinflation will force the Fed to cut more aggressively than currently priced
Rosie Macro Fund up 28% YTD vs S&P 500 +14% — going public as a listed ETF
trade_ideas: Long bonds on disinflation-driven Fed rate-cut narrative
Rosie Macro ETF launching as publicly listed vehicle

entries.summary: Yardeni raised his 2026 S&P 500 target to 8,250 (from 7,700) after a 'gangbusters' Q1 2026 earnings season, saying he 'wasn't bullish enough,' while maintaining his 'Roaring 2020s' thesis and long-term target of 10,000 by decade-end. He projects 3% GDP growth, 2.5% productivity gains, and $310 EPS for 2026, underpinned by the 'BRAIN Revolution' (Biotech, Robotics, AI, Nanotechnology). He expects only one Fed rate cut and views inflation as having peaked in 2025, moderating toward target. He sees 2026 theme as a rotation from AI producers to the 493 S&P companies that benefit from AI cost reduction.
key_views: 'Roaring 2020s' thesis intact — S&P 500 target raised to 8,250 after gangbusters Q1 earnings; 'wasn't bullish enough'
Long-term S&P 500 target: 10,000 by decade-end
BRAIN Revolution driving productivity: Biotech, Robotics, AI, Nanotechnology
2026 rotation: from AI producers to 493 AI user beneficiaries gaining from cost cuts and efficiency
Only 1 Fed rate cut anticipated in 2026; inflation peaked in 2025 and moderating
trade_ideas: Long S&P 500 broad index with 8,250 target
Rotate from AI producers (NVDA, SMCI) to AI user beneficiaries ('493' companies)

entries.summary: Goldman Sachs projects global GDP growth of 2.8% in 2026 (vs 2.5% consensus), with US growth accelerating to 2.6% as tariff headwinds fade and tax cuts kick in. Hatzius expects the Fed to pause in January 2026, then cut in March and June, reaching a terminal rate of 3-3.25%. Regional divergences are key: Germany rebounding strongly on fiscal stimulus, China growing at 4.8% but moderating on anti-involution reforms, euro area at a 'decent' 1.3%. Goldman forecast 'especially strong GDP growth in H1 2026.'
key_views: Global GDP forecast 2.8% (vs 2.5% consensus) — above-trend growth as tariff impact fades
US growth accelerating to 2.6% on tariff fade, tax cuts, and easier financial conditions
Fed: pause January 2026, cut March + June; terminal rate 3-3.25%
Germany: meaningful fiscal-driven rebound; China: 4.8% growth but moderating; euro area: 1.3%
'Especially strong GDP growth' expected in H1 2026

entries.summary: Wilson maintains his 'out-of-consensus' S&P 500 year-end target of 7,800, premised on a new bull cycle that started in April 2025 and is now broadening beyond tech. He projects 17% EPS growth driven by a 'rolling recovery' — manufacturing, housing, and lower-tier consumer goods sectors hitting their troughs at different times and now moving up in sync. This is 'The Bull Market's Underappreciated Narrative': the rally that began in tech is now bleeding into the broader economy, signaling a more inclusive and durable second act to the bull market.
key_views: S&P 500 year-end target 7,800 — 'out-of-consensus' bullish call maintained
New bull cycle started April 2025; entering 'second act' broadening beyond tech
17% EPS growth expected — driven by 'rolling recovery' across lagging sectors
'Rolling recovery': manufacturing, housing, lower-tier consumer hitting troughs and rebounding in sync
Bull market broadening is 'underappreciated narrative' — consensus too focused on tech concentration risk
trade_ideas: Long S&P 500 with sector rotation into manufacturing, housing, consumer discretionary

entries.summary: Napier maintains his thesis that we are entering a 15-20 year period of financial repression — governments structurally keeping interest rates below inflation to inflate away post-pandemic debt, paralleling the post-WWII experience. He argues central banks have become 'irrelevant,' now doing the bidding of indebted sovereigns rather than fighting inflation. Gold is signaling the coming regime shift. Napier warns yield chasing is dangerous and that technology will not defeat inflation; investors should favor real assets over bonds.
key_views: 15-20 year financial repression cycle beginning — structurally elevated inflation, rates kept below it
Central banks 'irrelevant' — now serving indebted sovereigns, no longer independent inflation fighters
Post-WWII parallel: governments will inflate away debt over decades via repression, not austerity
Gold signaling the inflationary regime shift — most reliable leading indicator
Technology will not defeat inflation — productivity gains insufficient vs fiscal dominance pressures
trade_ideas: Long gold as financial repression and inflation-regime hedge
Long real assets broadly; avoid long-duration bonds

entries.summary: Berezin's BCA Research sees 2026 unfolding in two distinct stages: H1 as a 'Great Rotation' from tech stocks to non-tech names, followed by a broad-based selloff in H2 driven by a weakening US economy. US recession probability stands at 40-50%. BCA separately argues that AI will 'kill the tech monopolies' by democratizing capabilities that previously only large platforms could offer, eroding competitive moats. Berezin maintains a structurally bearish view on US equities into H2 despite the early-year tech-to-value rotation thesis.
key_views: 2026 in two stages: H1 'Great Rotation' (tech → non-tech), H2 broad selloff on weakening US economy
US recession probability 40-50% — elevated, weighing on H2 outlook
AI will 'kill the tech monopolies' — democratizing capabilities erodes large-platform competitive moats
Rotate out of tech in H1 ahead of anticipated H2 broad market decline
Bearish structural view on US equities through H2 2026
trade_ideas: Rotate out of tech/Magnificent 7 into non-tech in H1
Reduce overall equity exposure ahead of anticipated H2 selloff

entries.summary: Sonders characterizes the 2026 environment as one of 'instability' rather than mere uncertainty — a distinction she finds important for portfolio construction. GDP growth is likely to stay slightly above the long-term 2-2.5% trend, supported by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act's fiscal stimulus skewed toward higher-income consumers and corporations. Inflation is expected to persist near 3%, well above the Fed's 2% target, with fixed income nevertheless offering solid returns as the Fed eventually cuts in response to labor market softening. S&P 500 earnings growth of ~25% is projected for the full year.
key_views: 'Instability' not just uncertainty — current environment demands more defensive portfolio construction
GDP growth slightly above 2-2.5% long-term trend, supported by OBBB fiscal stimulus skewed to higher-income cohorts
Inflation persisting near 3% — significantly above Fed's 2% target despite resilient economy
Fixed income: solid returns expected as Fed eventually cuts on labor market softening
S&P 500 earnings growth ~25% projected for full calendar year 2026
trade_ideas: Solid fixed income returns expected
Broad equity participation given 25% earnings growth backdrop

entries.summary: Bannister warns the market is underestimating three major risks: geopolitical tensions, weak consumer spending at lower-income cohorts, and dangerously concentrated tech exposure. He forecasts S&P 500 EPS growth decelerating to just 3.3% YoY by December 2026 ($262.50), far below Street consensus, and argues inflation will not slow to the low-2% range without a recession. The K-shaped economy is 'unsustainable' — affordability pressures on lower-income workers will become a political and economic crisis if not resolved.
key_views: Market underestimating 3 risks: geopolitical tensions, weak lower-income consumer, concentrated tech
S&P EPS growth decelerating to 3.3% YoY by December 2026 ($262.50 EPS) — far below consensus
Inflation will not slow to low-2% range without a recession
K-shaped economy 'unsustainable' — affordability crisis brewing for lower-income cohorts
Bearish contrarian vs. consensus bullish earnings growth forecasts for 2026
trade_ideas: Reduce concentrated tech exposure
Position defensively for earnings deceleration scenario

entries.summary: Visser, heading AI Macro Nexus Research at 22V Research, argues AI is simultaneously disrupting macro, crypto, and equities — creating factor rotations and leadership changes that traditional frameworks miss. He sees AI driving a structural rotation away from tech concentration toward value, commodities, and international markets, as AI democratizes capabilities that previously only mega-cap platforms could offer. His AI macro nexus framework integrates AI adoption rates and economic transformation dynamics for investors navigating the current inflection.

AI driving rotation from tech concentration to value, commodities, and international markets
AI reshaping tech leadership — new winners emerging outside traditional mega-cap platforms

Digital assets disrupted by AI just as equities are — crypto leadership also shifting
trade_ideas: Long value, commodities, and international equities as rotation from AI-concentrated tech

entries.summary: Mauldin frames the current economic environment as a 'Global Restructuring' — not a mere recession but a fundamental reorder that future observers will recognize as the moment everything changed, driven by geopolitical realignment, industrial policy shifts, and the AI transition. He characterizes the US economy as 'Bipolar': GDP averaged 2.7% YoY through March 2026 but showed extreme swings (Q4 2025 was 0.5% due to the government shutdown). Geopolitics is now the primary constraint on economics and international trade, making traditional macro frameworks increasingly unreliable.

He testified to the Senate on the economy's strength and told Fox Business that GDP growth 'with a three in front of it' is achievable by year-end. He explicitly rejected recession risk, forecasting the Iran energy shock will prove transitory and the Fed's 2% inflation target remains within reach, with his 'strategic economic realignment' thesis centering on directing capital toward national-security-critical sectors.

entries.summary: Einhorn called the US equity market 'the most expensive we've seen since we began managing money' (in 1996) and placed capital preservation at the top of Greenlight's priorities — a rare explicit signal last issued in a meaningful way before 2008.
