key_views: AI rally represents the biggest bubble since the railroad era
Current market structure echoes March 2000 dot-com peak with only 4% of S&P 500 at new highs
Global fund managers are paring risk holdings despite remaining positive on world growth
Stronger dollar is the most unexpected macro bet to position for
There is an imminent threat that could end the current stock market rally in June
trade_ideas: Stronger US dollar positioning
Safe, dividend-paying stocks rated by BofA Research
Long assets that would benefit from Iran peace deal (oil, defense, regional equities)

entries.summary: David Rosenberg warns of stagflationary pressures with inflation headed higher while growth decelerates. He notes that large parts of the economy are experiencing significant pain, suggesting deteriorating economic conditions beneath surface-level metrics.
key_views: Inflation is headed higher
Growth is headed lower
Large parts of the economy in deep pain
Monitoring Bank of Canada policy implications for markets

entries.summary: Yardeni maintains a bullish stance on US equities, arguing that current market strength is driven by solid earnings momentum (FEMO) rather than speculative bubble dynamics. He views recent market weakness as healthy corrections and remains optimistic on equities through 2028, while also highlighting the growing economic importance of the 50+ demographic segment.
key_views: US stock gains driven by earnings momentum, not bubble valuations
Market weakness and 'swoons' are healthy and normal developments
Aging population (50+) is an increasingly important economic force
Bull case for US stocks remains intact despite 'melt-up' concerns
FEMO (Fabulous Earnings Momentum) preferable to FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out)
trade_ideas: Long US equities based on earnings momentum thesis
Monitor gold prices at critical technical levels

entries.summary: Jan Hatzius maintains a cautiously optimistic near-term outlook with rate hikes remaining unlikely and U.S. recession risk cut to 15%, though he acknowledges darkening geopolitical risks from potential Iran conflict. He is optimistic on AI's growth impact despite European growth concerns, projecting continued U.S. economic expansion while Europe's growth slows significantly.
key_views: Rate hikes remain unlikely in the near term
U.S. recession risk is approximately 15%
U.S. economy will continue to grow
European growth will slow by approximately half
Optimistic about AI's positive impact on economic growth
Geopolitical risks from Iran conflict pose headwinds to global outlook
trade_ideas: Long U.S. equities relative to European equities given divergent growth trajectories
Long duration bonds given unlikely rate hikes

entries.summary: Mike Wilson sees US stock corrections as largely complete with fundamentals overriding AI doubts, though he anticipates another 5-7% downside before a bottom. He views recent market plunges as healthy resets positioning for fresh rallies, with interest rates identified as the final hurdle before equities move higher. Wilson also signals a significant shift away from traditional 60/40 portfolio allocations.
key_views: US stock market correction is largely done, with only 5-7% additional downside before bottoming
Fundamentals have overridden doubts around AI sustainability
Interest rates represent the final hurdle before equities can rally higher
Recent stock plunges represent healthy resets that set up fresh rallies
Sticky inflation remains a key concern affecting Fed policy outlook
Traditional 60/40 portfolio strategy requires significant structural shift
trade_ideas: Position for equity rally after rate clarity emerges
Reduce exposure to traditional 60/40 balanced allocations

entries.summary: Sonders sees 'red flags' and warns of complacency amid potential inflationary pressures, cautioning investors against casino-like behavior despite strong earnings and a rallying market. She flags risks from inflation, geopolitical tension pressuring consumers, and elevated equity valuations while monitoring rate path and bond yields as key risks.
key_views: Investors are dangerously complacent on major stock market risks despite record highs
Inflationary boom and pressure from geopolitical conflict pose headwinds to consumer spending
Market exhibiting 'casino-like behavior' with excessive speculation; rate path and bond yields are key risks
Strong earnings underpin rally, but valuations and macro headwinds warrant caution

entries.summary: Visser is constructive on equities amid market resilience and rising earnings that make valuations attractive on a forward basis. He remains unfazed by Bitcoin volatility and is actively buying dips, viewing crypto pullbacks as opportunities rather than warning signs. He sees AI mega-cap infrastructure plays (NVIDIA, Oracle, Amazon) and biotech (Eli Lilly at 55% growth) as key secular themes, with Ethereum emerging as a strategic asset class amid AI agent tokenization demand.
key_views: Market resilience signals a bull run; rising earnings make equities cheaper on forward multiples despite trillion-dollar market caps
Bitcoin weakness is tactical, not structuralâ€”charts show 'no hope' yet but still warrant accumulation at lower prices
AI capex cycle by mega-cap tech and cloud providers is driving a structural hedge surge and reshaping sector allocation
Ethereum positioned as critical asset as AI agents demand tokenized infrastructure
Biotech growth (Eli Lilly 55% growth) mirrors AI semiconductor scaleâ€”secular bull thesis
trade_ideas: Buy Bitcoin dipsâ€”viewing current weakness as accumulation opportunity
Overweight AI chip infrastructure (NVIDIA, Oracle, Amazon) over traditional software
Long Ethereum as AI agent adoption drives token utility demand
Long high-growth biotech (Eli Lilly) on NVIDIA-like growth trajectories

entries.summary: BofA's July 14 Global Fund Manager Survey shows investors have swung to their most bullish positioning since February 2026, even as Hartnett's Bull & Bear Indicator remains at a contrarian sell-signal 9.5. Hartnett articulates a 'Four Won'ts' consensus — no hard landing, no Fed hike before midterms, no AI capex cuts, no Democrat midterm sweep — and warns any single leg failing triggers a violent correction. He flags Japan bank stocks as a global 'canary': JGB yields near 3% and Japanese bank stocks tripled over three years, a signal that duration risk is being seriously mispriced elsewhere.
key_views: BofA FMS: investors most bullish since February 2026 — consensus and Bull & Bear at 9.5 sell signal diverging dangerously
'Four Won'ts' consensus — any single failure (hard landing, Fed hike, AI capex cut, Dem sweep) = violent correction
AI bubble = #1 tail risk for 45% of Fund Manager Survey respondents; AI capex at $800B in 2026, $1T in 2027
Japan bank stocks are the global 'canary' — JGB yields near 3%, bank stocks tripled in 3 years, signaling real duration risk
Investors remain 'frozen bullish' despite 5% long-bond yields — positioning not adjusting to yield signal
trade_ideas: 'Sleep like a baby' portfolio: 25% stocks / 25% bonds / 25% cash / 25% commodities

entries.summary: Goldman Sachs forecasts US growth cooling to 'stall speed' of just 1.25-1.75% in H2 2026 after June nonfarm payrolls came in at only 57,000 jobs — well below expectations and a second consecutive weak reading. Hatzius cut the 12-month US recession probability to 15% (from 25%) in June after an Iran peace deal removed the geopolitical risk premium. June CPI printed 3.5% annually, below the 3.8% consensus, as energy prices pulled back — though core services inflation and elevated Fed hawkishness under Kevin Warsh keep any near-term cut off the table.
key_views: US H2 2026 growth: 1.25-1.75% 'stall speed' — minimal buffer above recession territory, a downshift from the H1 2.6% call
June payrolls: only 57,000 jobs — second consecutive miss, labor market weakness becoming 'too entrenched' for cyclical reversal
Recession probability cut to 15% (from 25%) after Iran peace deal removed geopolitical risk premium (June 22)
June CPI: 3.5% vs 3.8% expected — energy pullback provides relief but core services remain sticky
Fed under Warsh: hawkish first meeting, higher-for-longer bias with rate hike back in play before year-end

entries.summary: Schwab's July 2026 Market Snapshot shows the bull market intact with 25% full-year S&P 500 earnings growth consensus, but Sonders highlights deepening fault lines: June CPI at 3.5% (below 3.8% expected) and June payrolls at only 57,000 jobs signal simultaneous slowing labor market and sticky inflation. Renewed US-Iran tensions have driven oil higher, acting as a supply-side shock on consumers already running thin on savings. The Great Moderation era of low volatility is over — Sonders argues the new regime of instability favors flexibility and diversification over passive buy-and-hold.
key_views: Bull market alive with 25% S&P earnings growth consensus but growth dangerously concentrated in tech (Alphabet, Broadcom, Micron, Sandisk, Intel)
June CPI 3.5% vs 3.8% expected — energy pullback provided temporary relief; core services inflation still elevated
June payrolls 57,000 — second consecutive miss; labor market cooling faster than headline GDP suggests
Renewed US-Iran tensions driving oil higher — new supply shock hitting households with low savings rates and weak real wage growth
Great Moderation over: new era of instability favors flexibility and diversification over passive exposure
